pmos

Marketing Measurement, Attribution & Modelling — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Marketing measurement is the work of figuring out which marketing actually worked — and it is far harder than it looks, because a sale almost never has a single, obvious cause. This primer maps the base layer every product manager should recognize: why measurement is so hard (the century-old "Wanamaker problem"), what attribution means, the simple rules-based models (first-touch, last-touch, linear, time-decay), multi-touch attribution (MTA), data-driven attribution and its Shapley-value math, the correlation-versus-causation trap that undermines all of them, incrementality and lift testing, geo experiments as the causal gold standard, marketing mix modeling (MMM), how MTA, MMM, and incrementality fit together, the privacy changes that broke user-level tracking, and the metrics and pitfalls to watch. The named camps disagree — especially on whether observational attribution can ever be trusted — but converge on one idea: only a controlled experiment proves cause, and everything else is a useful but fallible estimate. Written for any PM (product manager): every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across measurement vendors (Nielsen, Google, Haus, Measured, Triple Whale), the marketing-science canon, and Wikipedia's method definitions. 15 verified sources.

Why measuring marketing is hard (the Wanamaker problem)

The single most quoted line in all of marketing is a complaint about measurement. It is usually attributed to the department-store magnate John Wanamaker: "about one-half the money I spend for advertising is wasted, but I have never been able to decide which half." The quote is more than a century old, and the problem it names is still unsolved. Fittingly, even the quote's authorship is uncertain — Quote Investigator notes it "has been unable to find a direct citation… hence, this ascription remains uncertain."

Why is this so hard? Because a customer who buys something rarely does so because of one ad. They might have seen a billboard, heard a podcast spot, clicked a search ad, and asked a friend — all before purchasing. Marketing measurement is the discipline of untangling that web to decide what to spend more on and what to cut. Get it wrong and you defund the campaign that was actually working while pouring money into one that merely looked busy.

This primer walks through the main approaches in roughly the order the industry invented them: simple credit rules, then smarter statistical credit, then a hard pivot toward true experiments. A useful frame to carry throughout is the distinction between correlation (two things happened together) and causation (one thing actually caused the other). Almost every measurement failure in marketing is a case of mistaking the first for the second.

What attribution means

Attribution is the formal name for assigning credit. Wikipedia defines marketing attribution as "the identification of a set of user actions ('events' or 'touchpoints') that contribute to a desired outcome, and then the assignment of a value to each of these events." In plain terms: a "touchpoint" is any moment a customer encounters your marketing (an ad view, an email open, a click), and attribution decides how much each touchpoint gets credited for the eventual sale.

The reason attribution is a whole field, rather than a quick sum, is that the credit must add up to 100% across many touchpoints — and there is no single "correct" way to split it. Should the ad that first introduced the brand get the credit, or the search ad the customer clicked right before buying? Different rules give wildly different answers, and those answers directly drive budget decisions. Attribution is therefore less a measurement and more a choice of accounting rule.

It helps to hold one caution from the start: all attribution models, simple or sophisticated, work by watching which touchpoints appear in the paths of people who converted. That is an observation of what happened together, not proof of what caused what. Keep that limitation in mind — it is the thread that runs through every section below and the reason the field eventually turned to experiments.

Single-touch and rules-based models

The oldest attribution models are simple credit rules a person could compute by hand. Nielsen describes the two extremes: in a first-touch model "the first touchpoint… receives 100% of the credit," while in a last-touch model "the last touchpoint… receives 100% of the credit." First-touch flatters whatever introduced the customer; last-touch flatters whatever closed the deal. Both ignore everything in between.

Between those extremes sit two gentler rules. A linear (or even-weighting) model gives "all touchpoints… equal credit," and a time-decay model builds credit up toward the final touch, on the theory that recent interactions mattered more. The diagram below shows how the same five-touchpoint journey gets credited completely differently depending on which rule you pick.

Nielsen notes that last-touch became "the default marketing measurement methodology" for years, simply because it was easy. But every one of these rules is arbitrary — the credit reflects the rule's assumption, not any evidence about what changed the customer's mind. That arbitrariness is exactly what the next model set out to fix.

Multi-touch attribution (MTA)

Multi-touch attribution, almost always shortened to MTA, is the attempt to credit the whole journey rather than one point of it. Nielsen defines it as a technique that "takes all of the touchpoints… into consideration and assigns fractional credit to each." Instead of a single winner, every touchpoint gets a slice, and a marketer can see how much each channel contributed across many customers. The linear and time-decay rules above are simple forms of MTA; the harder forms try to learn the splits from data.

The appeal is obvious: MTA promises a fair, granular picture of which channels do the work, updated daily, at the level of individual users. Haus describes it as the method that "analyzes user-level digital interactions to assign credit across touchpoints." That user-level granularity is its strength — and, as later sections show, also its fatal weakness, because it depends on tracking individuals across the web.

There is a deeper catch that no amount of cleverness in the splitting rule can fix. Haus puts it bluntly: MTA, like its cousin marketing mix modeling, is "observational and only become[s] causal when paired with rigorous experimentation." MTA can tell you a touchpoint appeared on the path to a sale; it cannot tell you the sale would not have happened without it. That gap is the whole subject of the correlation-causation section below.

Data-driven attribution and the Shapley value

The most sophisticated form of MTA is data-driven attribution, the model Google made famous. Rather than apply a fixed rule, it learns the credit splits from your own data. Google describes it as using "all available path data—including data from both converting and non-converting users—to understand how the presence of particular marketing touchpoints impacts your users' probability of conversion." The key word is counterfactual: it asks what would have happened if a given touchpoint were removed.

The math underneath comes from an unexpected place — cooperative game theory. Data-driven attribution "uses an algorithm based on a concept from cooperative game theory called the Shapley Value," which, per Amsive, "was developed by the economics Nobel Laureate Lloyd S. Shapley as an approach to fairly distributing the output of a team among the constituent team members." Picture the channels as members of a team and the conversions as the team's output; the Shapley value asks how much each member adds across every possible lineup, and splits credit accordingly.

This is genuinely smarter than a fixed rule, and it is the best that observational attribution gets. But notice what it still is: a clever way of distributing credit among the touchpoints it can see, based on patterns in historical paths. It computes counterfactuals from observed data, not from a real experiment where ads were actually withheld. The next section explains why that distinction is the one that matters most.

The correlation–causation trap

Here is the single most important idea in marketing measurement. WITHIN states it cleanly: "Correlation = two things move together. Causation = one thing caused the other." Every attribution model — first-touch, last-touch, linear, time-decay, even Shapley-based data-driven attribution — measures correlation. It sees which touchpoints tend to appear before sales. It cannot, on its own, prove any of them caused the sale.

The most expensive symptom of this trap is last-touch bias. The channels that look most efficient are often just the ones that intercept customers who were already going to buy. As Northbeam illustrates, "if a paid social campaign generates 1,000 purchases, but the control group, which wasn't shown the ads, still produces 800 purchases, the incremental lift is only 200 purchases." A naive model would have credited the campaign with all 1,000 — overstating its true effect by five times.

This is the "organic baseline" problem: some customers would have converted anyway, and crediting the ad for them is an illusion. WITHIN warns that "in a complex media mix, relying on a single model oversimplifies reality" and leads to "wasted spend on channels that aren't driving conversions." The way out, the field eventually concluded, is to stop observing and start experimenting — which is what the next three sections are about.

Incrementality and lift testing

Incrementality is the part of an outcome that genuinely would not have happened without the marketing. Improvado defines marketing lift as "the measurable increase in a desired outcome… caused by a specific marketing campaign." The word "caused" is doing real work here — incrementality is the answer to the question attribution can only guess at. It measures net-new demand, not demand you would have captured anyway.

The method is borrowed straight from science: a controlled experiment. You split your audience into "an exposed group that sees your campaign, and a control group that doesn't," and the difference between them is the lift. Because the two groups are otherwise the same, anything that differs in their outcomes must be caused by the ads. This is the same logic as a drug trial, applied to advertising.

The arithmetic is simple. Improvado gives the formula: lift percentage equals "((Exposed Group Conversions − Control Group Conversions) ÷ Control Group Conversions) × 100." If the exposed group converts 2,000 times and the control 1,500, the lift is 33% — and, more usefully, 500 incremental conversions you can actually credit to the campaign. The catch is operational: running a true holdout means deliberately not showing ads to some customers, which feels like leaving money on the table and takes discipline to sustain.

Geo experiments: the causal gold standard

The cleanest way to run an incrementality test at scale is by geography rather than by individual. Triple Whale describes geo testing as a method that "works by running a controlled experiment across geographic regions" — you run ads in a set of treatment markets (cities, states, postal codes) and withhold them in control markets, then compare aggregate sales. Because whole regions are the unit, you never need to track a single person.

That property is why geo experiments have become the gold standard in the privacy era. As Triple Whale notes, the approach "doesn't rely on user-level identifiers or personal data, so it stays reliable through privacy shifts like ATT, cookie loss, or future regulation." When individual tracking collapses (the subject of the privacy section below), region-level measurement keeps working because it never depended on individuals in the first place.

The clever refinement is the synthetic control. Instead of comparing a treatment city to one arbitrary control city, the method builds "a weighted blend of control markets that mirrors how your treatment markets behaved before the test." If Los Angeles is the treatment market, its synthetic control might be mostly New York plus a few other cities whose combined pre-test sales tracked LA closely. That blended baseline is the counterfactual — the best available answer to "what would have happened anyway."

Marketing mix modeling (MMM)

Marketing mix modeling, shortened to MMM, takes the opposite approach to MTA: it ignores individuals entirely and works from aggregate history. Wikipedia defines it as "a statistical causal inference and forecasting methodology used to estimate the impact of various marketing tactics on product sales." Instead of tracking a person across touchpoints, MMM looks at total spend and total sales over time and asks which channels explain the ups and downs.

The technique is regression — fitting a statistical line through history. MMM "use[s] statistical models, such as multivariate regressions, and use[s] sales and marketing time-series data" to estimate each channel's contribution to sales volume and its return on investment (ROI, the revenue earned per dollar spent). Because it works on aggregate data, MMM can include channels that are impossible to track at the user level — television, radio, billboards, even weather and seasonality.

MMM's strengths and weaknesses are the mirror image of MTA's. It is privacy-proof and covers every channel, but it is coarse, slow, and data-hungry: Improvado notes it wants "100 weeks of historical data; 150+ weeks preferred" to separate seasonality from real media effects. It tells you roughly how to divide the budget across channels for next quarter, not which ad to tweak tomorrow. And, like MTA, it is fundamentally correlational unless calibrated against experiments.

MTA, MMM, and incrementality: a unified view

By now the three main methods are on the table, and the modern consensus is that they are not rivals but teammates. Measured assigns each a distinct role: incrementality "measures the causal impact of media using controlled test-vs.-control experiments," MMM "estimate[s] each channel's contribution to sales… but measures correlation — not causation," and MTA "tracks user-level digital journeys." Each answers a different question at a different altitude.

The practical rule of thumb is about fit, not superiority. Improvado advises using MMM "when offline channels represent more than 30% of total spend," the sales cycle "exceeds 30 days," or tracking is poor, and using MTA when "sales cycles are under 7 days" with high conversion volume. The diagram below places the three on the axes that matter — how causal each is, and whether it works at the strategic or tactical level.

The synthesis Measured recommends is called triangulation: run continuous experiments and let them calibrate the other methods, with "incrementality… as the causal foundation that calibrates broader measurement frameworks." In practice, as Improvado puts it, "mature teams run both: MMM sets quarterly budget envelopes, MTA drives daily campaign optimization" — and incrementality tests settle the arguments when the two disagree.

Privacy and the measurement crisis

The reason MTA fell from favor is not a better theory — it is that the data it needs has been disappearing. User-level attribution depends on following individuals across apps and sites, and a wave of privacy changes has made that nearly impossible. The two big shifts are Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT), which lets iPhone users refuse cross-app tracking, and the deprecation of third-party cookies, the small files that let advertisers recognize a browser across websites.

The damage is measurable. Admetrics reports that "platforms like Meta acknowledging a potential 15% underreporting of iOS web conversions due to iOS privacy regulations," and that "most browsers now restrict the lifespan of first-party cookies to between seven to 45 days, complicating long-term user tracking." When tracking windows shrink to a week, any customer who takes longer to decide simply vanishes from the data, and their initial touchpoints go uncredited.

This is why the field's center of gravity has shifted toward the aggregate, privacy-durable methods — MMM and geo-based incrementality — that never relied on individual tracking. Improvado notes that today "40-60% of tracking data is lost to privacy restrictions." The lesson for a product manager is structural: any measurement plan built on user-level tracking is now building on sand, and the durable answers come from experiments and aggregate models.

Metrics and common pitfalls

Underneath all these methods sits a layer of headline metrics every PM will hear. Lifesight defines marketing measurement as "the process of evaluating the effectiveness of marketing efforts by collecting and analyzing data… across various channels," anchored on a handful of numbers: ROAS (return on ad spend, revenue divided by ad cost), CAC (customer acquisition cost, the spend to win one customer), and ROMI (return on marketing investment). The trap is that all three can look great while telling you nothing about cause.

The pitfalls in this primer all trace to one root error — treating a correlational number as causal. Reporting a stellar ROAS from a last-touch model rewards demand capture and starves the brand-building that created the demand. Trusting platform-reported conversions ignores that each "walled garden" (Meta, Google) counts credit for itself with no shared view. And celebrating a metric that is not tied to a business goal — vanity over value — is its own quiet failure.

The discipline that separates good measurement from theater is insisting that important spend decisions be backed by an experiment, not just a dashboard. Lifesight's framing is that good measurement ensures "every marketing activity is directly tied to measurable financial outcomes." The honest practitioner accepts that perfect attribution is impossible, picks a few metrics that ladder to revenue, calibrates them against periodic lift tests, and resists claiming more precision than the data supports. Measuring imperfectly but honestly beats measuring precisely but wrongly.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

Marketing measurement sits at the intersection of marketing, statistics, and data analytics, so it borrows from and feeds into each. The topics below are the natural next steps once these fundamentals are in place — some deepen a single method above, others supply the wider context measurement operates inside. None are taught here; they are pointers to where the threads of this primer lead.

  • A/B testing and experimentation — the controlled-experiment discipline that incrementality and geo tests are a specialized form of.
  • Statistics and causal inference — the correlation-versus-causation foundation, plus confidence intervals and statistical significance that make a lift number trustworthy.
  • Growth marketing — the experiment-driven funnel optimization that consumes these measurement signals.
  • Performance vs brand marketing — the demand-capture-versus-demand-creation debate that last-touch bias distorts.
  • Product analytics — the in-product event tracking that complements channel-level marketing measurement.
  • Data privacy and consent — the regulatory and technical shifts (ATT, cookie deprecation, consent management) reshaping what is measurable at all.

References

  1. quoteinvestigator.com ·cited· (T1)

    The famous lament "about one-half the money I spend for advertising is wasted, but I have never been able to decide which half" is usually pinned on department-store magnate John Wanamaker, but Quote Investigator notes it "has been unable to find a direct citation… hence, this ascription remains uncertain" — the quote itself, traced to a 1919 speech, captures marketing's founding measurement problem.

  2. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    In marketing, attribution "is the identification of a set of user actions ('events' or 'touchpoints') that contribute to a desired outcome, and then the assignment of a value to each of these events" — the discipline of deciding which marketing moments get credit for a sale.

  3. nielsen.com ·cited· (T1)

    Nielsen defines the rules-based models cleanly: in first-touch "the first touchpoint… receives 100% of the credit," in last-touch "the last touchpoint… receives 100% of the credit," linear gives "all touchpoints… equal credit," and time-decay builds credit toward the final touch; multi-touch attribution instead "takes all of the touchpoints… into consideration and assigns fractional credit to each."

  4. support.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google's data-driven attribution "uses all available path data—including data from both converting and non-converting users—to understand how the presence of particular marketing touchpoints impacts your users' probability of conversion," computing each touchpoint's "counterfactual gains" — the conversion probability with the touchpoint present versus absent.

  5. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Marketing mix modeling (MMM) "is a statistical causal inference and forecasting methodology used to estimate the impact of various marketing tactics on product sales," using "statistical models, such as multivariate regressions" on "sales and marketing time-series data" to gauge each element's contribution, efficiency, and return on investment (ROI).

  6. haus.io ·cited· (T2)

    Haus contrasts the two workhorse methods — "MTA analyzes user-level digital interactions to assign credit across touchpoints, while MMM uses aggregated historical data to estimate the incremental impact" — and warns both share a limit: "both methods are observational and only become causal when paired with rigorous experimentation."

  7. amsive.com ·cited· (T2)

    Data-driven attribution "uses an algorithm based on a concept from cooperative game theory called the Shapley Value," which "was developed by the economics Nobel Laureate Lloyd S. Shapley as an approach to fairly distributing the output of a team among the constituent team members" — here the team members are channels and the output is conversions.

  8. within.co ·cited· (T2)

    WITHIN frames the core trap plainly: "Correlation = two things move together. Causation = one thing caused the other," and warns that "in a complex media mix, relying on a single model oversimplifies reality" — last-touch puts "100% of credit… on the final interaction" and so confuses correlation with cause.

  9. northbeam.io ·cited· (T2)

    Northbeam illustrates the organic-baseline problem: "if a paid social campaign generates 1,000 purchases, but the control group, which wasn't shown the ads, still produces 800 purchases, the incremental lift is only 200 purchases" — and stresses that "incrementality seeks to establish causality, not just correlation," unlike click-and-view attribution.

  10. improvado.io ·cited· (T2)

    Improvado defines marketing lift as "the measurable increase in a desired outcome… caused by a specific marketing campaign," measured by splitting audiences into "an exposed group that sees your campaign, and a control group that doesn't"; lift percentage = "((Exposed Group Conversions − Control Group Conversions) ÷ Control Group Conversions) × 100."

  11. triplewhale.com ·cited· (T2)

    Geo-based incrementality testing "works by running a controlled experiment across geographic regions" — treatment markets get ads, control markets don't — and is privacy-durable because it "doesn't rely on user-level identifiers or personal data"; advanced versions use a synthetic control, "a weighted blend of control markets that mirrors how your treatment markets behaved before the test."

  12. measured.com ·cited· (T2)

    Measured assigns the three methods distinct roles: incrementality "measures the causal impact of media using controlled test-vs.-control experiments," MMM "estimate[s] each channel's contribution to sales… but measures correlation — not causation," and MTA "tracks user-level digital journeys"; mature teams triangulate, with "incrementality… as the causal foundation that calibrates broader measurement frameworks."

  13. improvado.io ·cited· (T2)

    Improvado gives concrete selection thresholds: use MMM "when offline channels represent more than 30% of total spend," the sales cycle "exceeds 30 days," or identity resolution "falls below 60%"; use MTA when "sales cycles are under 7 days" with "over 1,000 conversions monthly" — and "mature teams run both: MMM sets quarterly budget envelopes, MTA drives daily campaign optimization."

  14. admetrics.io ·cited· (T2)

    Privacy changes are eroding attribution accuracy: "platforms like Meta acknowledging a potential 15% underreporting of iOS web conversions due to iOS privacy regulations," while "most browsers now restrict the lifespan of first-party cookies to between seven to 45 days, complicating long-term user tracking" — so click-based attribution increasingly undercounts delayed and cross-device conversions.

  15. lifesight.io ·cited· (T2)

    Marketing measurement is "the process of evaluating the effectiveness of marketing efforts by collecting and analyzing data… across various channels," anchored on metrics such as ROAS (return on ad spend), CAC (customer acquisition cost), and ROMI (return on marketing investment); good measurement ensures "every marketing activity is directly tied to measurable financial outcomes."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_marketing-measurement-attribution.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
